findings.name: Missing .gitignore
findings.description: No .gitignore file to prevent accidental commits
findings.guidance: Without .gitignore, sensitive files (.env, secrets.json, *.pem, *.key) can be accidentally committed to version control and exposed.

allFindings.name: Sensitive File Permissions
allFindings.description: Sensitive files have overly permissive permissions
allFindings.message: All sensitive files have appropriate permissions
allFindings.guidance: Overly broad file permissions let any user on the system read sensitive config files that may contain credentials or API keys.

allFindings.name: Missing .gitignore
allFindings.description: No .gitignore file to prevent accidental commits
allFindings.guidance: Without .gitignore, sensitive files (.env, secrets.json, *.pem, *.key) can be accidentally committed to version control and exposed.

allFindings.name: Cursor Rules Contain Credentials
allFindings.description: Cursor configuration files contain exposed credentials
allFindings.message: No credentials found in Cursor rules
allFindings.guidance: Cursor rules files are often committed to git. Credentials embedded there get pushed to remotes where anyone with repo access can extract them.

allFindings.name: VSCode MCP Config Credentials
allFindings.description: VSCode MCP configuration contains exposed credentials
allFindings.message: No credentials in VSCode MCP config
allFindings.guidance: MCP config files are shared across workspaces and often committed to repos. Credentials there are exposed to every tool and extension that reads the config.

allFindings.name: VSCode MCP Root Access
allFindings.description: VSCode MCP server has root or home directory access
allFindings.message: VSCode MCP filesystem access is scoped
allFindings.guidance: An MCP server with root or home directory access can read SSH keys, cloud credentials, and any file on the system. Scope access to the project directory only.

allFindings.name: Private Key Files
allFindings.description: Private key or certificate files found in project directory
allFindings.message: No private key files found in project root
allFindings.guidance: Private key files (.pem, .key) in a project directory are easily committed to git. Once pushed, the keys are compromised and must be rotated.

allFindings.name: Secrets in package.json
allFindings.description: package.json contains hardcoded secrets
allFindings.guidance: package.json is always committed to git and published to npm. Secrets there are visible to anyone who installs or forks your package.

allFindings.name: JWT Secret in Config
allFindings.description: JWT secret found hardcoded in configuration file
allFindings.guidance: A hardcoded JWT secret lets anyone who reads the config forge valid authentication tokens and impersonate any user.

allFindings.name: Executable Config Files
allFindings.description: Configuration files have executable permission
allFindings.message: No config files have executable permissions
allFindings.guidance: Executable config files can be run as scripts. An attacker who modifies a config file with execute permission can trick the system into running arbitrary code.

allFindings.name: Group-Writable Sensitive Files
allFindings.description: Sensitive files have group write permission
allFindings.message: No sensitive files have group write permission
allFindings.guidance: Group-writable sensitive files allow other users in the same group to modify credentials or inject malicious configuration values.

allFindings.name: Development Mode Enabled
allFindings.description: Development mode indicators found in configuration
allFindings.guidance: Development mode typically disables security features like CSRF protection, strict CORS, and error sanitization, leaving the application exposed in production.

allFindings.name: Debug Flags Enabled
allFindings.description: Debug or verbose logging flags are enabled
allFindings.guidance: Debug and verbose logging flags can leak internal state, database queries, and credential values into log files or console output.

allFindings.name: Verbose Error Messages
allFindings.description: Configuration enables verbose error messages
allFindings.guidance: Verbose error messages expose stack traces, file paths, and internal logic to attackers, making it easier to find exploitable weaknesses.

allFindings.name: Sensitive Data in Logs
allFindings.description: Potential sensitive data being logged
allFindings.message: No obvious sensitive data logging patterns found
allFindings.guidance: Passwords, API keys, and tokens logged to console or files persist in log aggregators and crash reports, where they can be harvested by anyone with log access.

allFindings.name: Log File Permissions
allFindings.description: Log files have overly permissive permissions
allFindings.guidance: World-readable log files let any local user read application logs, which often contain request details, internal errors, and sometimes credentials.

allFindings.name: Dependency Lock File
allFindings.description: No dependency lock file found
allFindings.message: No lock file found - dependency versions may vary between installs
allFindings.guidance: Without a lock file, npm install can resolve to different package versions on different machines, including versions with known vulnerabilities or supply-chain backdoors.

allFindings.name: Known Vulnerable Packages
allFindings.description: Package.json may contain known vulnerable packages
allFindings.message: No known vulnerable packages in direct dependencies
allFindings.guidance: These packages have confirmed supply-chain compromises (e.g., event-stream injected a cryptocurrency-stealing payload). Remove or replace them immediately.

allFindings.name: Wildcard Dependency Versions
allFindings.description: Package.json uses wildcard or latest versions
allFindings.message: All dependency versions are properly specified
allFindings.guidance: Wildcard (*) or "latest" versions accept any future release, including ones compromised by supply-chain attacks. Pin versions and use a lock file.

allFindings.name: Dangerous npm Scripts
allFindings.description: npm scripts contain potentially dangerous commands
allFindings.guidance: Scripts that pipe curl/wget to sh execute arbitrary remote code during npm install. An attacker who compromises the URL controls your build environment.

allFindings.name: Container User
allFindings.description: Dockerfile runs as root or has no USER directive
allFindings.message: Container runs as non-root user or no Dockerfile present
allFindings.guidance: A container running as root means any exploit that escapes the application gets full control of the container and potentially the host system.

allFindings.name: Claude Deny Rules
allFindings.description: No deny rules configured for Claude Code
allFindings.guidance: Without deny rules, Claude Code can execute any tool or command. Deny rules act as a blocklist to prevent dangerous operations like rm -rf or credential access.

allFindings.name: Claude Memory Persistence
allFindings.description: Claude memory persistence may store sensitive context
allFindings.guidance: Persistent memory can retain API keys, internal URLs, or confidential instructions across sessions. An attacker who gains access to the memory store can extract this data.

allFindings.name: Sensitive Instructions in CLAUDE.md
allFindings.description: CLAUDE.md may contain sensitive instructions that could be extracted
allFindings.message: No obviously sensitive instructions detected in CLAUDE.md
allFindings.guidance: CLAUDE.md is typically committed to version control. Sensitive instructions there can be extracted via prompt injection or by anyone with repo access.

allFindings.name: Tool Timeout Configuration
allFindings.description: No tool timeout configured for Claude operations
allFindings.guidance: Without tool timeouts, a stuck or malicious tool call can hang indefinitely, consuming resources and blocking the agent from responding.

allFindings.name: MCP Request Timeout
allFindings.description: No request timeout configured for MCP servers
allFindings.guidance: Without request timeouts, a hung or malicious MCP server can block the agent indefinitely, causing denial-of-service and preventing other tools from executing.

allFindings.name: MCP Retry Limits
allFindings.description: No retry limits configured for MCP servers
allFindings.guidance: Without retry limits, a failing MCP server can trigger infinite retry loops that waste API credits, saturate network connections, and stall the agent.

allFindings.name: MCP Localhost Binding
allFindings.description: MCP servers should bind to localhost only
allFindings.message: MCP servers properly bound to localhost
allFindings.guidance: MCP servers running over network (SSE/HTTP) without authentication let any network-adjacent attacker connect and issue tool calls.

allFindings.name: Sensitive MCP Tools
allFindings.description: MCP configuration includes potentially dangerous tools
allFindings.message: No obviously sensitive tool names in MCP config
allFindings.guidance: Tools named shell, exec, or eval typically provide arbitrary code execution. A prompt injection that invokes these tools can fully compromise the host system.

allFindings.name: MCP Logging
allFindings.description: MCP server logging configuration
allFindings.message: No explicit MCP logging configuration detected
allFindings.guidance: Logging MCP requests and responses creates an audit trail for detecting misuse. Without it, malicious tool calls leave no trace for incident response.

allFindings.name: Debug Endpoints
allFindings.description: Debug or admin endpoints may be exposed
allFindings.guidance: Debug and admin endpoints expose internal state, configuration, and metrics. Attackers use these to map your infrastructure and find weaknesses.

allFindings.name: WebSocket Security
allFindings.description: WebSocket connections may lack authentication
allFindings.guidance: Unauthenticated WebSocket connections let any client send commands to your backend. Unlike HTTP, WebSockets maintain persistent connections that bypass traditional request-based security.

allFindings.name: File Upload Security
allFindings.description: File upload without proper validation
allFindings.guidance: Unrestricted file uploads let attackers send malicious executables, web shells, or oversized files that can compromise the server or exhaust disk space.

allFindings.name: Query Parameterization
allFindings.description: Database queries may be vulnerable to injection
allFindings.guidance: Raw database queries built with string concatenation let attackers inject SQL or NoSQL commands that can read, modify, or delete all data in the database.

allFindings.name: Path Traversal Protection
allFindings.description: Potential path traversal vulnerability
allFindings.message: No obvious path traversal vulnerabilities found
allFindings.guidance: Path traversal (../) in file paths lets attackers read sensitive files like /etc/passwd or .env by escaping the intended directory.

allFindings.name: API Key in URL
allFindings.description: API keys may be passed in URL query parameters
allFindings.message: No obvious API key in URL patterns found
allFindings.guidance: API keys in URLs are logged by browsers, proxies, and web servers. They appear in referrer headers and browser history, making them easy to steal.

allFindings.name: Hardcoded Connection Strings
allFindings.description: Connection strings may be hardcoded
allFindings.guidance: Hardcoded connection strings contain database hostnames, ports, and credentials. Anyone with code access can connect directly to your database.

allFindings.name: Command Injection Protection
allFindings.description: Shell commands should use safe execution patterns
allFindings.message: Safe command execution patterns detected or no shell commands found
allFindings.guidance: Using exec() with user-controlled input lets attackers inject shell metacharacters (;, |, $()) to run arbitrary commands on the host system.

allFindings.name: Weak Cryptographic Algorithms
allFindings.description: Avoid using weak cryptographic algorithms
allFindings.guidance: MD5 and SHA1 have known collision attacks, and DES has a 56-bit key easily brute-forced with modern hardware. Data protected by these algorithms should be considered unprotected.

allFindings.name: Dangerous Tool Detection
allFindings.description: Identify potentially dangerous MCP tools
allFindings.guidance: Shell and exec tools give the AI agent arbitrary command execution on the host. A prompt injection can leverage these to exfiltrate data, install malware, or pivot to other systems.
